title: Why Care Minutes, Not Client Numbers, Decide Whether a Home Care Business Turns a Profit
linkedin:
Today is World Population Day, and it has us thinking about something we see constantly in home care and aged care businesses: client numbers are not the number that decides profitability.

Most providers we talk to are not short of referrals. An ageing population keeps demand strong. The number nobody is watching closely enough is care minutes, the gap between what a client's package funds and what it actually takes, travel, coordination and documentation included, to deliver the visit.

Two clients paying the same package fee can carry completely different margins once real time on the ground is measured against it. A client with complex needs or a longer drive between visits can quietly cost far more to serve than a simpler one generating identical revenue.

The providers managing this well have costed a visit properly, travel and coordination built in, and compared that real cost against what each funding tier actually brings in. Without that comparison, a full roster and a growing client list can mask a business that is steadily losing margin on its busiest days.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

#SMEFinance #ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
Home care and aged care providers rarely have a demand problem right now. Referrals keep coming. The tricky part is what happens after a new client signs on.

Two clients paying the same package fee can cost completely different amounts to actually serve once you count travel time, coordination and the extra minutes complex care needs. A growing client list can look like growth on paper while the real margin quietly slips underneath it.

Genuine question for anyone running a home care business: have you ever actually costed what a visit takes versus what it's funded for?

#HomeCare
google:
Today is World Population Day, a fitting moment to talk about something home care and aged care providers across Brisbane, Queensland, NSW and Victoria deal with every week: strong demand for care and a much trickier question underneath it about profitability.

Most home care businesses are not short of clients. An ageing population keeps referrals coming steadily. The harder question is whether each client is actually profitable once the real time to serve them, travel between visits, coordination, documentation, is weighed against what their funding package covers.

Two clients on the same package fee can carry very different margins depending on how much travel and complexity is involved in their care. A growing client list and a full roster can look like a healthy business while margin quietly erodes underneath it.

Providers who manage this well have properly costed what a visit actually takes, travel included, and compared that against what each funding tier brings in. That comparison, run properly and reviewed monthly, is usually the difference between a home care business that feels busy and one that can show exactly where its margin comes from.

If you run a home care or aged care business and have never run this comparison, it is worth doing before the next round of client growth. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
